title: Turkey Harasses EU Ministers Over Cyprus, Çiftçi Vows to Liberate Jerusalem, Venezuela Comes Calling
subhead: Turkish forces interfere with aircraft carrying Greek, French, and Dutch defense ministers en route to Cyprus hours before a France-Cyprus SOFA signing, while Interior Minister Çiftçi's Jerusalem remarks draw sharp Israeli rebuke and Venezuela's Delcy Rodriguez visits Istanbul for talks with Erdogan.

sections.heading: Turkish Forces Interfere With EU Ministers Over Cyprus
sections.body:
Turkish forces interfered with military aircraft carrying the defense ministers of Greece, France, and the Netherlands as they flew to Cyprus on June 8 for an informal EU Defence Ministers meeting in Nicosia. Radio communications between the aircraft and Larnaca air traffic control were disrupted by controllers operating from Ercan (Tymbou) airport in Turkish-occupied northern Cyprus, according to Cypriot and Greek officials.

Cypriot presidential press office director Victor Papadopoulos confirmed the government received reports from all three governments of interference, and that two Turkish F-16 fighter jets took off from Ercan and tracked at least one of the aircraft as it approached the island. All flights landed safely at Larnaca.

The Turkish Presidency's communications office called the harassment claims 'completely false,' stating that four of six aircraft heading from Greece to Cyprus had 'breached the airspace' of northern Cyprus, and that the F-16s were scrambled 'as a precaution.' Turkish Cypriot air traffic controllers' union leader Kursad Hudaverdioglu said the F-16s 'took off due to an emergency' and did not violate any borders.

Cypriot President Nikos Christodoulides called the interference 'unacceptable' and said it showed a 'revisionist approach.' Greek government spokesman Pavlos Marinakis said Athens 'unequivocally condemns this specific incident.' Cyprus plans to formally report the incident to the EU, and Defence Minister Vasilis Palmas will inform EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas and his European counterparts at the Nicosia summit.
sections.whyItMatters: The incident comes just hours before Cyprus and France sign a SOFA allowing French military deployment on the island — a deal the Turkish Cypriot side has declared null and void. With six Turkish F-16s and Hisar-A missiles stationed in northern Cyprus since March, the episode underscores how military deployments on the island are escalating tensions between Turkey and EU member states.

sections.heading: France-Cyprus Sign Military Deployment Agreement
sections.body:
France and Cyprus signed a Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA) on June 8 in Nicosia, establishing the legal framework for French military personnel to be stationed, train, and operate on Cypriot territory. The agreement was signed by French Armed Forces Minister Catherine Vautrin and Cypriot Defence Minister Vasilis Palmas.

The SOFA covers military coordination, interoperability, joint exercises, personnel exchanges, and defence industry collaboration. Cypriot President Christodoulides previously stated it would regulate the French military presence while respecting Cyprus' sovereignty.

The Turkish Cypriot administration condemned the deal as 'null, void, and legally invalid,' arguing that the Republic of Cyprus lacks authority to sign such agreements on behalf of the entire island and that it could alter the balance of power on the island. The agreement emerged from a visit to Cyprus by French President Emmanuel Macron and Greek PM Kyriakos Mitsotakis amid heightened regional security concerns following the Iran war and the deployment of F-16s to northern Cyprus.

The signing coincided with the informal EU Defence Ministers meeting in Nicosia, where discussions focused on regional security, Ukraine, maritime security, and Europe's defence readiness.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first formal SOFA between France and Cyprus, and it gives Paris a legal foothold for military forces in the Eastern Mediterranean at a time when Turkey has been building up its own military presence in northern Cyprus. The agreement is likely to deepen the strategic alignment between France, Greece, and Cyprus — and draw further pushback from Ankara.

sections.heading: Interior Minister Çiftçi Vows to Liberate Jerusalem
sections.body:
Turkish Interior Minister Mustafa Çiftçi declared at a ruling party convention in Çorum province that Turkey will one day reclaim control over Jerusalem, drawing a sharp diplomatic rebuke from Israel.

Çiftçi stated that just as Turkey witnessed the 'liberation' of Damascus, Aleppo, and Karabakh, it will witness the 'freedom of Jerusalem.' He added that during his tenure as governor of Erzurum province, he prayed to serve even a single day as the governor of Jerusalem, and that he believes this day will come. He also asserted that territories previously under Ottoman rule would return to Turkish sovereignty, calling President Erdogan a 'global leader.'

After 24 hours of silence, Israel's Foreign Ministry responded: 'Wake up and smell the coffee. The corrupt Ottoman Empire is gone. Forever. The capital city of Jerusalem will remain Israel's eternal capital.' Defence Minister Israel Katz added that Jerusalem has been the capital of the Jewish people for 3,000 years, calling the Ottoman Empire envisioned by Çiftçi and Erdogan 'collapsed and will never return,' and criticised Erdogan's legacy as contradicting that of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk.

The remarks follow a pattern of escalating rhetoric from Turkish officials toward Israel since October 2023, including Erdogan describing Israel as a 'terror state' and Turkey's Istanbul prosecutor indicting 35 senior Israeli officials over the navy's seizure of the 'Sumud' flotilla bound for Gaza.
sections.whyItMatters: Çiftçi's remarks are the most expansive neo-Ottoman claim from a senior Turkish minister in recent memory — going beyond criticism of Israeli policy to explicitly asserting that formerly Ottoman territories should return to Turkish sovereignty. The incident adds another strain to Turkey-Israel relations and feeds into the regional narrative around the France-Cyprus SOFA, where Turkey's position in the Eastern Mediterranean is increasingly contested.

sections.heading: Venezuela's Delcy Rodriguez Visits Istanbul for Talks With Erdogan
sections.body:
Venezuela's acting president Delcy Rodriguez arrived in Istanbul on June 8 for talks with President Erdogan, according to multiple sources cited by Middle East Eye. Rodriguez, who has been in India discussing energy, trade, and investment with the Modi government, is stopping in Istanbul on her return trip.

The visit comes amid a significant shift in Venezuela's political landscape. Former president Nicolas Maduro was abducted by US forces in January and transferred to a detention centre in New York to face a federal criminal case. Rodriguez took over as acting president with the blessing of the Trump administration, and has since overseen an expansion of US interests in Venezuela, including those of oil company Chevron.

Turkey and Venezuela have strong political ties. Erdogan maintained a close rapport with Maduro, who was one of the first leaders to call Erdogan after the 2016 coup attempt. The two countries' energy ministers signed two memorandums of understanding in February 2024 covering oil, natural gas, and mining. Maduro also announced an agreement for Turkey to extract gold from fields in southern Venezuela.

However, heavy US sanctions on Venezuela's energy and mining sectors previously prevented deeper cooperation. Earlier this year, the Trump administration eased some of those sanctions, potentially opening the way for Turkish investment in Venezuelan oil and gold. Sources told MEE that Ankara may be particularly interested in those sectors.
sections.whyItMatters: Rodriguez's visit signals that Turkey is positioning itself as a commercial partner in post-Maduro Venezuela at a time when the US has eased sanctions. The convergence of US sanctions relief and Turkish interest in Venezuelan oil and gold could give Ankara new leverage in energy markets at a time when the Hormuz closure has disrupted global supply chains.

sections.heading: Economy Slows as Q1 Growth Misses Forecasts, CBRT Set to Hold Rates
sections.body:
Turkey's GDP grew 2.5% year-on-year in Q1 2026, down from 3.4% in the prior quarter, and well below expectations. On a seasonally adjusted basis, the economy expanded just 0.1% quarter-on-quarter, worse than every estimate in a Bloomberg survey whose median was 0.3%.

The slowdown comes as the Iran war continues to disrupt energy markets. Turkey imports most of its oil and natural gas, making it particularly vulnerable to higher global energy prices. The central bank reversed its easing cycle earlier this year, raising a key interest rate to contain market volatility from the broader emerging-market selloff triggered by the conflict.

Official CPI inflation edged up to 32.61% y/y in May from 32.37% in April. The central bank sharply revised its year-end inflation forecast to 26% from a previous range of 15-21%, citing elevated energy and food prices. According to Bloomberg, the next rate-setting meeting is scheduled for June 11, with economists generally expecting the CBRT to hold the policy rate at 37%. Capital Economics' William Jackson noted that pressure from the energy import bill and political turmoil had raised the possibility of a hike, but the recent dip in oil prices and the weak GDP data make that move 'somewhat less likely.'
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's economy is feeling the Iran war's impact through energy costs and inflation stickiness. The Q1 miss — 0.1% quarterly growth — is a warning signal that tight monetary policy and external shocks are biting harder than expected. With the CBRT meeting on June 11 and inflation stuck above 32%, the policy dilemma is acute: hold rates and risk lira depreciation, or hike and further squeeze growth.

sections.heading: CHP Crisis Continues as Özel Draws Thousands in Ankara Protest
sections.body:
Turkey's opposition crisis deepened after a court ruling annulled the 2023 CHP congress that elected Özgür Özel as party leader, effectively removing him from office. The ruling, which also reinstated former leader Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu, has been widely seen as a politically motivated move to weaken the main opposition party.

Özel drew thousands of supporters to an Ankara protest on May 30, demonstrating he retains significant grassroots backing. According to Reuters, the crowd size showed Özel can still command strong support despite the court ruling. But the crackdown has been forceful: riot police fired tear gas and forced their way into CHP headquarters to evict the ousted leadership on May 24, and further detentions have followed.

Protests have spread beyond Ankara. In İzmir, police used water cannon against demonstrators. The CHP has vowed to pursue legal appeals. Analysts describe the ruling as a test of Turkey's balance between democracy and autocracy, and the episode has rattled financial markets already under strain from the Iran war's economic fallout.
sections.whyItMatters: The court-ordered removal of Turkey's main opposition leader — and the use of riot police to enforce it — represents the most serious judicial intervention against a political party in Turkey in years. With the NATO summit in Ankara approaching in July, the optics of a crackdown on the opposition while hosting alliance leaders will draw international scrutiny.

bottomLine: June 8 brought a cluster of developments that underscore Turkey's increasingly assertive posture in the Eastern Mediterranean and beyond. Turkish interference with aircraft carrying three EU defense ministers to Cyprus — hours before France and Cyprus signed a military deployment agreement — signals that Ankara will not accept a growing French-Greek-Cypriot alignment passively. Interior Minister Çiftçi's Jerusalem remarks, the sharpest neo-Ottoman claim from a senior Turkish official in recent years, drew immediate Israeli condemnation and complicate Turkey's regional diplomacy. Venezuela's acting president arriving in Istanbul for talks with Erdogan points to a commercial opening in Venezuelan oil and gold as US sanctions ease. At home, the economy grew just 0.1% quarter-on-quarter in Q1, well below forecasts, with inflation stuck above 32% and the CBRT facing a rate decision on June 11. The CHP opposition crisis continues to simmer, with Özel drawing large crowds but the court ruling and police enforcement of his removal unchanged. The common thread: Turkey is simultaneously projecting power outward — in Cyprus, in its rhetoric on Jerusalem, in its outreach to Caracas — while facing mounting economic and domestic political pressures that constrain its options.
sources: Politico, Cyprus Mail, Greek Reporter, Greek City Times, Israel National News, Middle East Eye, Reuters, Bloomberg, ThePrint, DevDiscourse, Anadolu Agency, Al Jazeera, Capital Economics, MSN
